message.content:
You are verifier #2, independent of all other verifiers. Do NOT confirm findings optimistically — only confirm if you can independently verify the issue exists in the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.

Finding F-017: "O(R * N) resample stability computation in pure Python — no vectorisation path"
File: src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py (line 190)
Description: _resample_stability() calls _empirical_p(null, r, tail) once per resample statistic. _empirical_p iterates full null_distribution O(N) per call. R=1000, N=10000 -> 10M Python comparisons. No numpy path.
PoC: R=1000, N=10000: 10 million Python comparisons in stability loop alone.

Instructions:
1. Read the actual file at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py
2. Look at the code at line 190 and surrounding context
3. Independently verify whether the described problem EXISTS in the current code
4. Check if the PoC is reproducible from the actual code as written
5. Consider whether this is already fixed (pass-1 #101-#122, pass-2 #124-#156 fixed many issues)
6. Return your independent verdict

Confirmed = true ONLY if: (a) the code problem exists as described, (b) the PoC is reproducible from current code, (c) it is a genuine production-readiness concern. Set confirmed=false for speculative issues, issues already fixed, or issues where the PoC does not match what the code actually does.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

178	def _resample_stability(
179	    resamples: Sequence[float], null: Sequence[float], tail: Direction, alpha: float
180	) -> float:
181	    """Fraction of resamples that **reproduce the observed effect**.
182	
183	    Each resample is scored as its own observed statistic against the null and must itself be
184	    significant in the predicted tail (empirical p ≤ ``alpha``). This measures reproduction of
185	    the observed *effect* — not merely landing on the predicted side of the null center, which a
186	    resample distribution clearing the null median by an epsilon would satisfy while reproducing
187	    nothing of the observed magnitude (audit pass 2, #139). The closer to 1.0, the more
188	    consistently the effect's significance reproduces across resamples.
189	    """
190	    significant = sum(1 for r in resamples if _empirical_p(null, r, tail) <= alpha)
191	    return significant / len(resamples)
192	
193	
194	_VALID_DIRECTIONS = ("greater", "less")
195	
196	
197	def _resolve_direction(hypothesis: Hypothesis, predicted_direction: Direction | None) -> Direction:
198	    """Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24); the confirm-time
199	    argument is optional and, if given, must match the pre-registered one.
200	
201	    The resolved direction is validated against ``{"greater", "less"}`` and any other value is
202	    rejected: an unrecognized direction would otherwise fall through to the 'less' tail in
203	    :func:`_empirical_p`, silently flipping supported↔contradicted (audit pass 2, #128).
204	    """
205	    locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
206	    if locked_dir is not None:
207	        if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
208	            raise ValueError(
209	                "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
210	                f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
211	            )
212	        resolved = locked_dir
213	    elif predicted_direction is None:
214	        raise ValueError(
215	            "no predicted_direction: set it on the hypothesis at pre-registration "
216	            "(preferred) or pass it explicitly"
217	        )
218	    else:
219	        resolved = predicted_direction
220	    if resolved not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS:
221	        raise ValueError(
222	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got {resolved!r} — an "
223	            "unrecognized tail would silently flip the supported/contradicted verdict"
224	        )
225	    return resolved
226

content.text: Now let me look at `_empirical_p` to confirm it does O(N) work per call.

content.content:
160	        p_value=p_value,
161	        alpha=alpha,
162	        powered=powered,
163	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
164	        test_description="held-out unit-level test",
165	    )
166	
167	
168	def _empirical_p(null: Sequence[float], observed: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
169	    """One-sided empirical p-value with +1 smoothing for the given tail."""
170	    n = len(null)
171	    if tail == "greater":
172	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed)
173	    else:
174	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v <= observed)
175	    return (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)

content.text: The finding is confirmed. The code at line 190 calls `_empirical_p(null, r, tail)` once per element in `resamples` via a generator expression. `_empirical_p` (lines 168-175) iterates the entire `null` sequence in pure Python with a generator comprehension — O(N) per call. With R=1000 resamples and N=10000 null distribution values, this is exactly 10 million Python comparisons. There is no numpy vectorization path (no `np.searchsorted`, no `np.sum(null >= observed)`, nothing). The PoC matches the code exactly.

input.reasoning: The code at line 190 of /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py calls _empirical_p(null, r, tail) once per element in the resamples sequence inside a generator expression. _empirical_p (lines 168-175) performs a full O(N) scan of the null distribution using a pure-Python generator comprehension (sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed) or the less-tail equivalent). With R=1000 resamples and N=10000 null values, this produces exactly 10 million Python-level comparisons. There is no numpy vectorization path anywhere in either function — no np.searchsorted, no np.sum with boolean masking, no numpy at all. The PoC described matches what the code actually does. This is a genuine production-readiness concern: sorting the null once and using np.searchsorted (or computing np.sum(null_arr >= r) for each r via broadcasting) would reduce this to microseconds instead of seconds for typical R/N values.
